speaker: interviewer
text: S-P-E-I-C-H-E-R, and this is roll number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about what happens when the Germans formed the ghettos in your hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghettos-- they-- they formed two ghettos in two different parts of town. The ghettos were not enclosed. And the part of town where I lived, we were moved into-- I don't know how to call it. It was like a big building where they used to be for factory workers, like in a company town. And it was like single, one-room apartments. And we were pushed into that, and some neighboring homes. So we got one room. We had to leave whatever we have yet. All the furniture and everything else, we had to leave behind, because we couldn't take anything with us. We just took whatever clothes we called and a few towels or sheets or whatever, and we moved in there. We moved in there. And of course there was a lot of people yet left. And life went on. Mothers tried to cook whatever they had to cook with to feed the family. Used to try to get some bread once in awhile, we used to get, and potatoes and so on. And we used to go to work. The mothers stayed home, and we went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do, Marion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in a cabinet maker shop, because I used to watch him always. So I knew a little bit about it, and that's what I went to work. And my father worked there, and I worked there. And it was in a different part of town, but we used to walk there in the morning, come back home in the evening, home to the ghetto. And life was miserable. We were always living in constant fear and not knowing when and what and how is going to happen. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was going to work, passing by headquarters of the Ukrainian police, and I saw a big gathering of the Ukrainian police. And I immediately noticed that something is wrong. I ran home to warn my mother and all the other neighbors that they're probably going to be an action. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and truthfully, a few hours later, it started. At that time my mother was still able to hide. So did my aunt, and so were some other people. But a lot of people were taken away, and they were sent to Belzec where they had the crematoriums. That was the beginning of the crematoriums in Belzec. When they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Belzec. Belzec. B-E-L-Z-E-T-Z. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they didn't take too many people at that time away. And we still survived, and we lived through the winter, which wasn't that bad, till about February of 1943. In 1943, there was an action that lasted for three days. They took away, at that time, my mother and my grandmother, and about 600 other people. They kept them in the movie house for about a week, in inhuman conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's name was Dora, D-O-R-A. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My grandmother lived most of the time-- all the time, actually-- with my aunt and uncle. At that time she was taken away. My mother was taken away. As I said, they kept them in inhuman conditions for about a week. Then they took them all out. We had a slaughterhouse outside the town. And in front of that slaughterhouse, they dug a tremendous grave, one massive grave. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took the 600 people. Everyone had to get undressed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go over to the edge of that grave, and they were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you told about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know about it almost immediately because-- because it happened in town. And at that time, we still had Jewish police, which was working with the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the Germans, I would say, or for the committee-- Jewish committee. They were ordered to form a Jewish police, which were coordinating things with the Germans. Some of our Jewish policeman, truthfully, behaved very badly. They used to be as bad as-- almost as the Germans, except they had no arms. They just had a whip or a nightstick, whatever you want to call it. And they used to-- Jews were very ingenious. They used to build things-- false walls in houses, false basements, and things to hide in. And our Jewish policemen, in many instances, used to know about it because they lived with the Jews and they heard about things. They heard where their families used to hide. And they used to uncover these things and take people out, just trying to survive themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unfortunately, very few of them survived, or fortunately. Because many of them were very bad people. I'll tell you a story about some of them on the end of our concentration camp stories. But right now, where my mother was shot, then they liquidated the ghetto and my part of town, where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that, after they took away the 600 people, and they took-- a lot of people die out from hunger, and there was no need for them to keep the Jews in two places. So one ghetto was closed up, which we called liquidated, and we were all went into the second part of town, to one ghetto. There was a very small amount of people left already. Maybe there was 2,000, 3,000 people left, not even then. And at that time, we still kept on working. My mother wasn't there anymore. My sister was hidden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was hidden in a place-- we had a power station [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the Russians, when they were running away in 1941, they blew it up, so there was no electricity in town. But that power station evidently had some underground. I was never there and never seen it. But there was quite a few people, maybe 50, 60 people hidden there. And my sister was one of them. The German knew-- the Germans knew about it, but they couldn't find the entrance to it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so it was only my father and me in that ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But one night-- we knew-- we knew that it's not going to last forever. And we used to have guards-- our people, myself, all the young people-- we used to sit on the perimeter of the ghetto at night for a few hours, changing guards, to see whether the Germans are surrounding the ghetto. And truthfully one night, they did surround the ghetto. And my father and myself were taken away. We were taken away with a few hundred, maybe 500, 600 people-- other people-- to a gathering place which was behind the headquarters of the Ukrainian police. We're sitting there. The Germans were all around those with dogs, with those vicious dogs. And all of a sudden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the head engineer of town of Boryslaw, of our town, came there. He was a Ukrainian, very nice gentleman. And he saw my father. And he had the privilege of taking a few people out, so he took my father out. And my father told him that I am there, and they took-- they released me too. Even though the German was squawk-- the officer in charge of the assessment was squawking a little bit, but he did let me go. The other people, of course, were taken out and destroyed-- shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that went on for-- that was already 1943. And after a short while, they closed off, they liquidated the ghetto. And at that time they issued [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a card, which was called an R. It's an identification card, which was called R, for the [GERMAN] industry, which means for the armament industry. And whoever got that R was relocated to another big building that used to be occupied by very poor workers. And they had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at that place they had horses. They used to be employed in the oil industry, because our roads were very bad. And that's where they settled the rest of the Jews that were still officially working. And between those people, there was a few that didn't have that R. And I was, unfortunately, one of them that didn't have it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at one point, at one action, we were also taken. I'm getting a little bit mixed up with the-- with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] times of when what happened. At one time, my father and me were taken away to a neighborhood town, which was by the name of Drohobych. That was our sister town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're going to ask me to spell it, right? D-R-O-H-O-B-Y-C-H. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were taken to work there at a place where they make-- where they dig clay and they make bricks. I don't know what you call a place like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, my father was taken away to work at a home of a very high SS officer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was put pushing lorries with that clay. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw that I will not last long. I will last-- after 10 days, I picked myself up. I ran away. I went back to my hometown, to Boryslaw. And I went to that place where the rest of the Jews were working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I more or less hid there. I got lost there for a little while. I could not get that R identification card. I just had no connections. And that's why I couldn't officially be there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that time, I had a friend that was hidden by a Christian family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somehow I got in touch with her. And I went in to that family, which was only a mother and daughter. They were very poor, dirt poor. The whole apartment consisted of one room with a little pantry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of m in every pantry in our neighborhood, in our town, in our part of Poland, there was a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cave-- not cave. It was a little hole in the ground where you used to use for refrigeration in the summer, and in the winter. Because in the winter it kept things warm, and the summer kept things cool. Anyway, I started enlarging that thing under the floor, and I built a hole of maybe-- I don't know, maybe six by 10, and maybe four feet high. You could not stand up. And I was hidden there for a few months. At the same time, I went back to see my father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I asked him to come back, to be hidden with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that unfortunate time, also, they uncovered the hiding place where my sister was. But it so happened that that day, when they uncovered them and they took them all to be shot, she was not there that day. She went to that place where all the people were working. That was called [POLISH], [GERMAN] in German. In Polish was [POLISH]. Let's call it a labor camp. Let's call it this way. She was there for that day. And I took my father and my sister with me. But because we had no money-- we had very little-- and the people were very poor that were hiding us. They really couldn't feed us. So whatever we had, we used to sell-- a extra pair of pants, or whatever. I don't remember what we had already-- we sold. And there came to a point where we had nothing else to sell. So my father and my sister voluntarily went to that camp, because there was no way to survive. There was no place to hide. A lot of people were hiding in the forests. We had tremendous forests all around us where the Germans didn't dare to go in. But our wonderful Ukrainians that lived all around in the villages, they spotted them, and they used to uncover them, and they bring them out. And especially bad it was in the winter. In the winter, when the snow was high and these people had to come out to get some food and water, they used to see the-- the footprints in the snow. But yet, some of these people survived in the forest, through the whole-- some of them up to two years. They dug big trenches, they covered it up, and somehow they survived. But we had nobody. I didn't know anybody in those places. They didn't want to take any more people, because food was-- great shortage of food. That was the main problem, was food. And-- and I was still on and off hidden in that place. Then one day, in broad daylight, I had a last pair of pants to sell. So I was wearing two pair of pants on me. I was walking the street. And to tell you the truth, I did not look Jewish. The Jew was considered with black hair, with a big nose, and so on and so forth. I wore no armband. I walked through the streets. And all of a sudden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like he came from under earth, an SS man, with a gun in my face. And he says, [GERMAN] hands up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he slapped my face, and he took me to that camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that time, I gave out-- I gave up already. I was resigned already. I had no way-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no way how to-- to survive. While in that camp, I must tell you that, in April of 1944, there was one transport of people from that camp taken away to a concentration camp near Cracow, which was called Plaszow. I'll spell it to you. It's P-L-A-S-Z-O-W. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was right on the outskirts of Cracow. And a transport from our work camp was taken there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the Germans, for whatever reason, and also the chief of our Jewish police at that time, was taken away to that Plaszow concentration camp. A few months later, the Germans brought him back from that concentration camp to show to the rest of the Jews proof that they're not being killed. They're only take-- being taken to work in a concentration camp. And truthfully, he did come with them to tell people that people weren't killed. They were taken to a concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He also knew, at that time, who was hiding in the forest and who was not. He had his information. And when that German SS man brought me in, he knew I wasn't hiding in the forest. Because if you were hiding in the forest, they used to beat you till you tell them where the people were hidden. And if you didn't tell them, they killed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you didn't look Jewish. How do you think he knew that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody must have pointed me out. That was the only way, till today, that I can explain. Because it was in a part of town that I don't think anybody knew me. But you never know. Somebody must have passed by and pointed me out to him, because I lived later as a Pole, and no-- between Polacks. And nobody recognized that I was a Jew. But that was much later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, he brought in, and that chief of Jewish police said that I wasn't in the forest. I don't know anything about it. They let me go. And I stayed in that camp without working for about a month. How I survived, I don't know. What I ate, there must have been some food. That time my father came in there and my sister. And we were all three there, resigned to whatever is going to happen is going to happen. There-- we had no choice. Our civilian population didn't help. As a matter of fact, at that time already, we could hear the big guns of the front, which was only a few hundred miles away. And we expected the Russians to come in any time because they were pushing the Germans back already at great speed, till they stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone there able to listen to a radio at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There must have been people that had radios, or they had Christian friends that had radios. But you could hear the front. And at one point, they stopped, I guess to regroup-- the Russians-- or whatever. And that was the killer of another few thousand Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? They stopped a few hundred miles away from us, where if they could have-- would've been going without stopping, maybe in a week they would have reached us. Anyway, on-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on one day-- I must tell that story-- I was hanging around or standing around not too far from the gate. An SS man came running. He needs four guys with a stretcher-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four guys with a stretcher. He went-- I-- he grabbed me, and I went. And not too far away on a field there was laying a friend of ours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the German-- from the army, from the Wehrmacht-- was watching him. He shouldn't run away. And we had to-- the German claimed that he was wounded, and we had to put him on the stretcher and bring him to camp. On the way, we spoke to him, and he said he is not wounded, but they caught him with a gun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They caught him with a gun, and they brought him to camp. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] While he was in camp, they put him-- and they had a little jail there-- they put in that jail.
